# The importance of resilience in global supply chain management

[**Webinar**: Real Tactics for Supply Chain Shocks](https://www.orkestrascs.com/webinar/whats-working-now-real-tactics-for-supply-chain-shocks)

Jonas Mehrhoff

Resilience, the buzzword that has gained increasing relevance as global supply chains continue to be tested more than ever before. But what does it really mean? What does resilience look like in global supply chain management? More importantly, why is it so important for a supply chain’s success? In this article, we aim to answer just that.

## What is supply chain resilience?

Supply chain resilience refers to the ability of a supply chain to anticipate, adapt to, and recover from disruptions swiftly and effectively while maintaining continuous operations. It involves the capacity to withstand and bounce back from various challenges, such as natural disasters, economic fluctuations, geopolitical changes, supplier issues, or unexpected events like the recent COVID-19 pandemic.

A resilient supply chain is characterized by its ability to:

1. **Anticipate Risks:** Identifying potential disruptions and risks in advance to proactively prepare for them.
2. **Adapt Quickly:** Swiftly adjusting strategies, processes, and resources to mitigate the impact of disruptions.
3. **Recover Rapidly:** Implementing recovery plans and actions to resume operations as soon as possible after a disruption.
4. **Maintain Operations:** Ensuring continuity in delivering products or services to customers without significant interruptions.

Building supply chain resilience involves implementing strategies such as diversifying suppliers, creating backup plans, investing in technology for visibility and agility, and fostering strong relationships with partners. The goal is to create a robust and flexible supply chain that can navigate challenges without compromising efficiency or customer satisfaction.

## Importance of a resilient global supply chain

Events such as COVID-19 and the blockage in the Suez Canal, choking trade flows on both sides of the canal for six days, exposed to the world how fragile global supply chains truly are. As global supply chains involve hundreds of stakeholders, depending on the type of industry, and a myriad of activities, it is inherently prone to disruptions. Disruptions in supply chains can occur in various forms ranging from economic, trade, political, climate and digital, all of which negatively impact day-to-day operations, leading to increased costs and lead times.

Supply chain disruption can be due to companies:

* Not being prepared
* Not flexible and agile
* Not having a wide range of suppliers
* Not having supply chain visibility
* Not being digitally enabled

Supply chain disruptions that didn’t garner much attention in the past have become more probable in recent years. Not only private supply chains, but even governments around the world seem guilty of being unprepared for the recent disruptions, with McKinsey citing, “The US was heavily reliant on other countries for a wide range of products including products that were critical and crucial to the American consumer and industry.” It revealed that the US, like many other developed countries in the western world, sourced a broad range of essential goods from Wuhan, where COVID-19 is said to have originated. These goods include key pharmaceutical ingredients and antibiotics, masks and personal protective equipment, and automotive parts, all of which were in extremely short supply when the pandemic hit the rest of the world.

Data from UN Comtrade showed that:

* China and India exported 42% of global antibiotic ingredients with China exporting 75% of streptomycin and 52% of penicillin
* Taiwanese exports of electronic integrated circuits (semiconductors) account for 43% of global value
* Taiwan and South Korea produce most of the world’s advanced chips
* China exports three-quarters of all personal computers and two-thirds of all mobile phones globally
* In total, around 180 products with a value of around $134 billion are exported almost exclusively by a single country

Naturally, supply chain disruptions from any of these countries could lead (and have led) to global bottlenecks. Companies and countries alike are waking up to a harsh reality of the necessity of building resilience in their global supply chains to sustain themselves. Governments have taken steps to review national supply chains while private sector companies have also gone back to the drawing board to enhance their supply chains to create resilience.

Governments in India, Australia and Japan have learnt some harsh lessons from the pandemic; that dependence on a single source of supply is incredibly risky. To mitigate these risks these countries announced the creation of the [Resilient Supply Chain Initiative](https://www.dfat.gov.au/news/media-release/australia-india-japan-economic-ministers-joint-statement-supply-chain) (RSCI) highlighting the need for regional cooperation on supply chain resilience in the Indo-Pacific region. As part of this, they have encouraged businesses to spread out their production plants to other countries in the region. This enables them to diversify procurement and reduce the risk of supply chain disruption affecting all their suppliers.

Another example of supply chain disruption due to unpreparedness is the congestion issue at US ports which has caused steep increases in ocean freight rates, severe container shortages, and unhappy customers. While the current issues may be due to the increased demand by the US customer, it is also being attributed to the lean method of working in the Just in Time supply chain model, which seems to have been rendered invalid in the current situation.

## Actions to make global supply chains more resilient

Global supply chains are [complex networks](https://www.orkestrascs.com/blogs/supply-chain-management-crash-course-what-you-need-to-know) and any failure in one link creates vulnerability throughout the chain. Companies need to create and nurture resilience in their supply chains so they can be proactive, rather than being reactive in addressing these vulnerabilities. Actions that can be taken to create and nurture resilience in supply chain include:

* Understanding and analysing the risks that your supply chain faces both upstream and downstream, and developing mitigation strategies
* Using [data analytics](https://www.orkestrascs.com/blogs/importance-of-analytics-in-supply-chain-and-its-growth) effectively to monitor and manage risks from a centralized location, avoiding duplication and ambiguity
* Diversifying your operations so that you’re not reliant on a single source. As global volatility increases, it’s wise to explore other options to protect your supply chain when disruptions occur
* Creating end-to-end [visibility](https://www.orkestrascs.com/downloads/supply-chain-visibility-the-whole-picture) with real-time information and automated system-driven push notifications to be informed immediately if a discrepancy occurs anywhere in your supply chain
* Building close and trusting partnerships with suppliers and service providers in the supply chain in order to jointly support each other and find solutions to disruptions

A resilient supply chain allows companies to adapt to global events proactively and have the capability to adjust schedules to accommodate customer requirements. This includes being able to collaborate with vendors and customers effectively using integrated systems including adjustments made to the supply chains for better control.

Related:

[The ultimate guide to supply chain visibility.](https://www.orkestrascs.com/downloads/supply-chain-visibility-the-whole-picture)

## Next steps in supply chain resilience

While the digital transformation of global supply chains has been on the cards for the last few years, COVID-19 and its impact have accelerated the pace at which the industry is transforming, forcing supply chain leaders to reimagine and reengineer their supply chains quicker. As [Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/en/supply-chain/trends/future-of-supply-chain) states, “while most supply chains in the past were designed for cost efficiency, the future supply chains will have to balance resilience, agility, cost-effectiveness, speed all aimed at [customer satisfaction in supply chain](https://blog.orkestrascs.com/importance-of-customer-satisfaction-in-supply-chain).” This impact has challenged supply chain leaders to advance the digital agenda of their supply chains and build resilience to face the potential disruptions of the future.

Related:

[Learn more about how to acheive resilience with a digital platform versus an ERP.](https://www.orkestrascs.com/downloads/comparing-an-erp-extension-to-a-digital-supply-chain-platform)

## Conclusion

By understanding the importance of crafting a plan that works for your specific network to see your goals come to fruition, you will ultimately achieve true supply chain resilience. By creating a clear picture of your supply chain and the risks involved, leveraging data and analytics to get ahead of your problems, and exercising transparency and communication both internally and externally across your network, your supply chain can achieve solid resilience now and for the future. Supply chain resilience is the key to a smooth-running supply chain, and the answer to get you out of that constant problem-solving loop. With these practices in mind, what once seemed like a lofty goal isn’t so out of reach.

SUMMARY:
Resilient supply chains are better equipped to identify potential risks and develop strategies to mitigate them. Moreover, resilient supply chains allow companies to respond swiftly to emerging risks, minimizing potential damages and demonstrating adaptability in a dynamic landscape. Customer satisfaction is the bedrock of business success, and resilient supply chains play a pivotal role in maintaining this satisfaction. The resilience of a company's supply chain has a direct impact on stakeholder confidence. > *Unilever, a multinational consumer goods company, has earned stakeholder confidence through its* *commitment to sustainable and resilient supply chains*. FULL CONTENT:
Navigating the intricate tapestry of today's global economy, supply chains serve as the crucial conduits linking businesses to resources, markets, and consumers. In an era defined by unprecedented challenges and unforeseen disruptions, the intricate web of global supply chains has emerged as a critical lifeline for businesses across industries.

From sourcing raw materials to delivering finished products, supply chains are the arteries that keep commerce flowing. Yet, the vulnerability of these networks to external shocks has never been more apparent.

> *Natural disasters, geopolitical tensions, and economic uncertainties—all have the potential to disrupt supply chains, underscoring the need for resilience.*

In this dynamic landscape, the concept of a resilient supply chain has evolved from a mere strategy to an imperative. Building a supply chain that can not only weather storms but thrive amid them has become a hallmark of successful businesses.

In this article, we delve into eight pivotal advantages that resilient supply chains offer, illuminating why they stand as pillars of enduring business success.

###### IN THIS POST:

[1. Risk Mitigation](#risk-mitigation)

[2. Enhanced Agility](#enhanced-agility)

[3. Reduced Lead Times](#reduced-lead-times)

[4. Improved Customer Satisfaction](#improved-customer-satisfaction)

[5. Cost Efficiency](#cost-efficiency)

[6. Innovation Promotion](#innovation-promotion)

[7. Stakeholder Confidence](#stakeholder-confidence)

[8. Regulatory Compliance and Sustainability](#regulatory-compliance-and-sustainability)

> #### RELATED:
>
> ##### [HOW DIGITIZATION IMPROVES SUPPLY CHAIN EFFICIENCY, AGILITY AND CUSTOMER FOCUS](/en/blog/how-digitization-improves-supply-chain-efficiency-agility-and-customer-focus?hsLang=en)

1. Risk Mitigation

Resilient supply chains are better equipped to identify potential risks and develop strategies to mitigate them. This proactive approach enables businesses to anticipate challenges before they escalate into full-blown crises.

By diversifying suppliers, locations, and transportation routes, organizations can reduce their vulnerability to disruptions. This strategic diversification not only enhances risk distribution but also ensures continuity even in the face of unexpected events.

Moreover, resilient supply chains allow companies to respond swiftly to emerging risks, minimizing potential damages and demonstrating adaptability in a dynamic landscape.

2. Enhanced Agility

The agility fostered by resilient supply chains transcends mere adaptability. It empowers businesses to navigate challenges proactively and seize opportunities with finesse. The ability to swiftly realign resources and processes according to shifting market dynamics is a hallmark of supply chain resilience.

An agile supply chain can quickly pivot its production, distribution, and sourcing strategies in response to changes in customer preferences, technological advancements, and regulatory shifts. This flexibility not only minimizes disruptions but also positions businesses to capitalize on emerging trends, gaining a competitive edge in the process.

> *An excellent example of a company benefiting from an agile supply chain is Zara, a Spanish fashion retailer. Zara is known for its fast fashion model, where it can quickly respond to changing fashion trends.*

By maintaining a highly responsive supply chain, Zara can design, produce, and deliver new clothing styles to stores within weeks. This agility not only keeps Zara's product offerings fresh but also allows the company to capitalize on emerging trends and outpace competitors.

*By maintaining a highly responsive supply chain, Zara can design, produce, and deliver new clothing styles to stores within weeks.*

3. Reduced Lead Times:

Shortened lead times are a direct outcome of a resilient supply chain. By optimizing processes and fostering close collaboration with suppliers, businesses can achieve quicker turnaround times from order placement to product delivery.

This efficiency not only enhances customer satisfaction but also allows companies to respond promptly to changes in demand.

The ability to adjust production schedules and inventory levels swiftly based on real-time market feedback ensures that businesses can cater to evolving customer preferences while minimizing the risk of overstocking or stockouts.

> #### RELATED:
>
> ##### [4 KEY FACTORS OF CHOOSING AN AUTOMATION SYSTEM FOR WAREHOUSES](/en/blog/4-factors-to-consider-when-choosing-an-automation-system-for-warehouse-operations?hsLang=en)

4. Improved Customer Satisfaction:

Customer satisfaction is the bedrock of business success, and resilient supply chains play a pivotal role in maintaining this satisfaction. Reliable and consistent product availability, coupled with timely deliveries, forms the cornerstone of a positive customer experience.

A resilient supply chain ensures that disruptions are minimized, reducing the chances of unexpected delays or shortages. The result is a higher level of trust and loyalty among customers who can rely on a company to fulfill their needs consistently.

5. Cost Efficiency

While building supply chain resilience requires initial investments, the long-term cost savings are substantial. Resilient supply chains streamline processes, reduce inefficiencies, and minimize the financial impact of disruptions.

By proactively addressing potential risks and designing flexible response strategies, businesses can avoid costly emergency measures such as expedited shipments, last-minute sourcing, and excess inventory storage. Over time, these cost-saving measures contribute to healthier bottom lines and enhanced operational efficiency.

> *Apple Inc. has demonstrated the cost efficiency that can arise from a resilient supply chain. Apple sources components for its products from various suppliers across the globe. In 2011, following the earthquake and tsunami in Japan, Apple's diversified supply chain helped it avoid significant disruptions that affected many other tech companies.*

By spreading its sourcing across different suppliers and regions, Apple minimized the risk of shortages and maintained production without incurring excessive costs associated with delays.

*In 2011, following the earthquake and tsunami in Japan, Apple's diversified supply chain helped it avoid significant disruptions that affected many other tech companies.*

6. Innovation Promotion

Resilient supply chains encourage a culture of innovation within organizations. The pursuit of resilience often leads to the adoption of advanced technologies and data-driven strategies. Integration of IoT devices, real-time monitoring systems, and predictive analytics enhances visibility across the supply chain.

This heightened visibility enables proactive issue identification and resolution, enabling businesses to take data-driven decisions that optimize performance and mitigate risks.

> #### RELATED:
>
> ##### [HOW TO INNOVATE YOUR BUSINESS BY CHASING DIGITALIZATION? HERE'S HOW...](/en/blog/innovate-your-business-digitalization?hsLang=en)

7. Stakeholder Confidence

The resilience of a company's supply chain has a direct impact on stakeholder confidence. Investors, partners, and customers are more likely to trust and engage with businesses that demonstrate proactive risk management and contingency planning.

Companies that communicate their commitment to supply chain resilience showcase their ability to navigate uncertainties, fostering stronger relationships with stakeholders. This, in turn, bolsters brand reputation and positions the company as a reliable and forward-thinking player in the market.

> *Unilever, a multinational consumer goods company, has earned stakeholder confidence through its* *commitment to sustainable and resilient supply chains*.

Unilever's Sustainable Living Plan includes ambitious targets for reducing environmental impact and enhancing social welfare across its supply chain.

This proactive approach not only aligns with societal expectations but also appeals to socially conscious investors and partners who view the company as a responsible and reliable player in the market.

*Unilever's Sustainable Living Plan includes ambitious targets for reducing environmental impact and enhancing social welfare across its supply chain.*

8. Regulatory Compliance and Sustainability

Resilient supply chains align with regulatory requirements and sustainability goals. By conducting thorough risk assessments and developing comprehensive risk mitigation strategies, companies are better positioned to identify compliance gaps and environmental risks.

The ability to promptly address these concerns not only ensures regulatory adherence but also contributes to sustainable business practices. Resilient supply chains often incorporate strategies that reduce waste, promote ethical sourcing, and enhance overall environmental stewardship, aligning with societal expectations and regulatory mandates..

In an era characterized by uncertainty and rapid change, the significance of resilient supply chains cannot be overstated. The benefits outlined in this article demonstrate that investing in supply chain resilience is not merely a proactive measure but a strategic imperative.

By building a resilient supply chain, businesses can safeguard their operations, enhance customer satisfaction, and position themselves for growth in an ever-evolving global marketplace.

As the saying goes, "It's not about avoiding the storm, but about learning how to dance in the rain." Building a resilient supply chain equips businesses to do just that.

URL: https://www.bcg.com/publications/2020/resilience-in-global-supply-chains

SUMMARY:
ByBen Aylor,Bitan Datta,Megan DeFauw,Marc Gilbert, Claudio Knizek, andMichael McAdoo Exactly what a future resilient supply chain looks like will vary by industrial sector, location, and the type of sourcing and manufacturing network that best fits each organization’s strategic objectives. Where individual companies and products within each industry segment land on the impetus to change/ease of adjustment matrix will vary, of course, depending on the markets they serve or their supply chain configurations. The first step to improving resilience is to gain a clear view of supply chain risks at the company, business segment, or product level, depending on which level is like to be actionable for a given organization.

What is supply chain resilience?
--------------------------------

Supply chain resilience refers to a supply chain's ability to anticipate, adapt and recover from disruptions, such as natural disasters, pandemics or other unexpected events.

Resilient supply chains maintain[continuity](https://www.ibm.com/topics/business-continuity)by minimizing the impact of disruptions and ensuring customer satisfaction. Key pillars of supply chain resilience include contingency, flexibility, visibility and collaboration. These pillars allow businesses to address vulnerabilities and adapt quickly to evolving circumstances.

Technologies such as[artificial intelligence](https://www.ibm.com/topics/artificial-intelligence)(AI),[machine learning](https://www.ibm.com/topics/machine-learning)(ML),[blockchain](https://www.ibm.com/topics/blockchain)and[digital twins](https://www.ibm.com/topics/what-is-a-digital-twin)can enhance supply chain resilience. They can also provide real-time insights that improve decision-making,[operational efficiency](https://www.ibm.com/topics/operational-efficiency)and supply chain networks.

Why is supply chain resilience important?
-----------------------------------------

In today’s interconnected world,supply chain risks are inevitable. The COVID-19 pandemic revealed the vulnerabilities of global supply chains and how quickly disruptions can cause cascading effects across industries. For example, manufacturers of[semiconductors](https://research.ibm.com/blog/what-are-semiconductors)faced shortages and delays for critical raw materials, severely impacting global production of cars, smartphones and gaming systems.[](https://www.ibm.com/topics/sustainability?_gl=1*3opu4l*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTMzMzYuMC4wLjA.)

Since then,supply chain leaders have recognized the need to prioritize resilience. And yet, according to a McKinsey survey, many respondents are still struggling to effectively manage supply chain risks, with significant gaps in their ability to mitigate them.[1](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/supply-chain-resiliency#1)

Building resilient supply chains provides several key benefits for businesses, including:

*   **Improved business continuity**:A resilient supply chain ensures that operations continue to run smoothly despite disruptions, reducing the risk of shutdowns. Businesses can maintain procurement and functionality, optimize inventory management, minimize downtime and enhance customer satisfaction.

*   **Better risk mitigation**:By proactively identifying risks and implementing strategies to mitigate them, companies can avoid severe disruptions. [Predictive analytics](https://www.ibm.com/topics/predictive-analytics?_gl=1*e7oln*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTI5OTcuMC4wLjA.) and AI tools can help businesses anticipate potential problems and adjust [sourcing](https://www.ibm.com/topics/sourcing?_gl=1*1nmr24l*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTMzMzQuMC4wLjA.) or production strategies before they affect operations.

*   **Enhanced agility and flexibility**:Resilient supply chains are agile, enabling businesses to adjust to changes quickly. Diversifying suppliers and nearshoring operations can reduce the risks associated with relying on single sources or distant regions.

*   **Cost reduction**:Although initial investments in resilient supply chain technologies might be high in the short term, they can lead to cost optimization in the long term. By reducing waste, optimizing lead times and reexamining [procurement](https://www.ibm.com/topics/procurement?_gl=1*1ajiesx*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTM0NDAuMC4wLjA.) strategies, businesses can lower overall costs and improve profitability.

*   **Sustainability and compliance**:Resilient supply chains often incorporate [sustainability](https://www.ibm.com/topics/sustainability?_gl=1*3opu4l*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTMzMzYuMC4wLjA.) practices, such as [ethical sourcing](https://www.ibm.com/topics/ethical-sourcing?_gl=1*1y3g2wz*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTM0MzguMC4wLjA.) and [renewable energy](https://www.ibm.com/topics/renewable-energy?_gl=1*1rzk9ql*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTM0MzkuMC4wLjA.). These practices can reduce environmental impact, improve brand reputation and meet stakeholder demands for responsibly sourced products. They can also help ensure compliance with regulations such as the [Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive](https://www.ibm.com/topics/csrd?_gl=1*1drrgse*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTM0NDAuMC4wLjA.) (CSRD).

The four pillars of resilient supply chains
-------------------------------------------

Resilient supply chains typically exhibit four core components: contingency, flexibility, visibility and collaboration.

 Contingency 

Contingency involves establishing backup processes and maintaining extra inventory to ensure business continuity during disruptions.

 Flexibility 

Flexibility allows businesses to quickly adjust their supply chain operations to accommodate the unexpected.

 Visibility 

Visibility is crucial for identifying and addressing potential disruptions.

 Collaboration 

Collaboration fosters strong relationships between internal teams and external partners, which is critical for effective supply chain management.

Supply chain resilience strategies
----------------------------------

To strengthen supply chain resilience, companies can consider a combination of proactive planning, technology adoption and operational flexibility. Often, resilience strategies focus on critical supply chain practices such as [forecasting](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/forecasting),[inventory management](https://www.ibm.com/topics/inventory-management),[supplier management](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supplier-management)and commodity management.

### Forecasting

Effective demand[forecasting](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/forecasting)requires visibility across the entire supply chain to anticipate demand, identify risks and proactively manage disruptions. Many organizations invest in advanced [demand planning](https://www.ibm.com/topics/demand-planning) and forecasting tools to improve[supply chain management](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supply-chain-management), optimize[safety stock](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/safety-stock)and respond to market fluctuations. For example, companies can explore tools such as AI-driven demand forecasting or blockchain for improved supply chain transparency and predictive insights.

Additionally,a comprehensive risk management plan—regularly updated with input from key stakeholders—can help businesses identify, prioritize and respond to potential supply chain risks more effectively.

### Inventory management

In response to longer lead times and increased supply chain disruptions, businesses are reimagining their inventory management strategies. For instance, many retailers are shifting from just-in-time (JIT) models, which emphasize efficiency, to just-in-case(JIC) models, which prioritize safety stock. This tactic can help businesses gain flexibility and avoid stockouts during demand fluctuations.

Companies are also adopting automated[inventory management](https://www.ibm.com/topics/inventory-management)tools to mitigate supply chain bottlenecks and ensure material availability. Using real-time data analytics, businesses can dynamically adjust inventory levels in response to shifting market conditions.

### Supplier management

Strong, collaborative[supplier relationships](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supplier-management)are essential for mitigating supply chain risks. Systems that continuously monitor supplier performance metrics (such as quality, delivery and capacity) can help identify issues early and maintain a high level of service from suppliers. Companies can also pursue diversification. Strategically diversifying an ecosystem of partnerships through multisourcing and nearshoring can minimize the impact of regional or geopolitical disruptions, improving flexibility.

Finally, organizations can use supplier data to manage risks: Establishing shared data platforms with suppliers and[logistics](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supply-chain-logistics)providers allows for early detection of potential risks. These platforms enable companies to proactively address issues before they escalate.

### Commodity management

Effective commodity management improves[supply chain risk management,](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supply-chain-risk-management)maintains production continuity and enhances overall supply chain performance—even during periods of instability. For example, companies can use hedging strategies or long-term contracts to stabilize costs and reduce the financial impact of price volatility.

Metrics for supply chain resilience
-----------------------------------

To measure the effectiveness of a resilient supply chain, companies can track key performance [metrics](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supply-chain-analytics). Three key metrics are:

### Time-to-survive

Time-to-survive refers to the length of time a business can continue to operate during a supply chain disruption without experiencing severe operational or financial setbacks. This metric measures the ability of a supply chain to endure disruptions, such as shortages or delays, while still [fulfilling](https://www.ibm.com/products/order-management) customer orders and maintaining [business continuity](https://www.ibm.com/topics/business-continuity).

### Time-to-recover

Time-to-recover is the period that it takes for a business to return to normal operations following a disruption. The quicker a business can recover, the less impact a disruption will have on the[customer experience](https://www.ibm.com/topics/customer-experience)and overall performance. Reducing time-to-recover is essential for maintaining a competitive advantage and ensuring that businesses remain resilient in the face of unexpected events.

### Time-to-thrive

Time-to-thrive is the duration it takes for a supply chain to not only recover but also capitalize on opportunities after a disruption. A resilient supply chain does not just return to normal; it positions the business to grow postdisruption. Businesses that thrive after disruptions are often those that adapt quickly, innovate and refine their processes in the aftermath of a crisis.

Challenges to building supply chain resilience
----------------------------------------------

Despite the clear advantages of resilience, companies face several challenges when attempting to build and maintain resilient supply chains:

*   **Complexity and globalization**: Managing a global supply chain can be challenging due to the increased risks associated with geopolitical instability, transportation delays and natural disasters. As supply chains expand across borders, the complexity of[risk management](https://www.ibm.com/topics/risk-management)and visibility increases, making resilience harder to maintain.

*   **Lack of end-to-end visibility**:Without complete visibility into the entire supply chain, businesses can struggle to identify and address disruptions quickly. Siloed systems and outdated infrastructure can cloud supply chain visibility and prevent companies from gaining real-time insights into supply chain performance, limiting their ability to act proactively.

*   **Technological integration**: Many businesses still lack the infrastructure to implement[advanced technologies](https://www.ibm.com/products/mro-inventory-optimization)such as AI, ML and blockchain at scale. Integrating these technologies into existing supply chain systems can be complex, requiring significant investment and a shift in operations.

*   **Cost and resource constraints**: The upfront costs of building a resilient supply chain, including investments in redundancy,[automation](https://www.ibm.com/topics/automation)and advanced technologies, can be prohibitive. However, the long-term benefits—such as improved forecasting, reduced disruptions and greater agility—usually outweigh the initial costs.

Technologies for building supply chain resilience
-------------------------------------------------

Businesses are using various technologies to help build supply chain resilience:

### AI and ML

AI and ML are transforming supply chain management by enabling [predictive analytics](https://www.ibm.com/topics/predictive-analytics?_gl=1*10g0j33*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTQ1OTkuMC4wLjA.), [demand forecasting](https://www.ibm.com/topics/demand-planning?_gl=1*1eqrdu2*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTQ2NTcuMC4wLjA.) and real-time decision-making. These technologies can help businesses anticipate disruptions, [optimize inventory](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/inventory-optimization?_gl=1*1h08uk0*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTQ2NTguMC4wLjA.) and accelerate the [digital transformation](https://www.ibm.com/topics/digital-transformation?_gl=1*11jt1dp*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTQ2NjAuMC4wLjA.) of their supply chain.

### Blockchain

[Blockchain](https://www.ibm.com/topics/blockchain)technologies can enhance[supply chain transparency](https://www.ibm.com/topics/supply-chain-transparency), providing a secure and immutable record of transactions. This ledger can help improve traceability, reduce fraud and increase trust across the supply chain—especially in industries with strict compliance standards.

### Digital twins

A [digital twin](https://www.ibm.com/topics/what-is-a-digital-twin?_gl=1*y28q7n*_ga*MjAzNjM0NjQ2MC4xNzMyNzAxODY0*_ga_FYECCCS21D*MTczMzQ5MjUyNC4xNy4xLjE3MzM0OTQ2NjIuMC4wLjA.) can provide a virtual representation of a supply chain, allowing businesses to simulate scenarios and test strategies without disrupting operations. This technology can help businesses identify potential vulnerabilities and optimize processes for greater resilience.

### Internet of Things

[Internet of Things](https://www.ibm.com/topics/internet-of-things)(IoT) devices enable real-time monitoring of inventory, equipment and environmental conditions, offering valuable insights into supply chain performance. By connecting these devices to enterprise systems, businesses can react quickly to potential disruptions.

### Cloud computing

[Cloud](https://www.ibm.com/topics/cloud-computing)platforms provide scalable, flexible solutions for managing supply chains. These platforms allow for real-time data access, integrate seamlessly with other technologies and can help improve collaboration across global supply chains.

### Robotic process automation

[Robotic process automation](https://www.ibm.com/topics/rpa)(RPA) automates repetitive tasks, improving operational efficiency and reducing errors. By automating routine processes, businesses can respond more quickly to supply chain changes and reduce the risk of disruptions.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Identifying potential disruptions, such as natural disasters or geopolitical tensions, enables businesses to proactively prepare and mitigate impacts on supply chains.
Implementing strategies like diversifying suppliers and enhancing visibility helps organizations swiftly adapt and recover from unexpected disruptions, ensuring continuous operations.
Building resilient supply chains fosters sustainable practices by reducing waste and ensuring compliance with regulations, ultimately enhancing brand reputation and stakeholder trust.
A resilient supply chain allows businesses to respond quickly to changes, ensuring customer satisfaction and enabling them to seize market opportunities ahead of competitors.
Organizations must prioritize resilience by investing in technology, training, and adaptive strategies to navigate future challenges and maintain operational continuity.
